Each project is included to demonstrate product reasoning, clear architecture, or high-standard frontend execution.

fleetscan.name: FleetScan
fleetscan.tagline: Fleet incidents, field scan, manager oversight
fleetscan.role: Product design, frontend architecture, Supabase integration, authentication, and scan workflow ownership.

meta.title: FleetScan - Case study
meta.description: Fleet incident operations app with QR flow, role model, RLS, and strict validation.

paragraphs: Field teams need to report a truck status in seconds, without friction and without unstable manual entry.
Managers need a reliable fleet view without heavy enterprise tooling or fragile shared spreadsheets.
Mobile-first workflow: scan by QR or plate, open the vehicle sheet, and make a binary report (damage vs nothing to report).
Lean Supabase backend: SSR auth, role-scoped RLS, versioned SQL migrations, and strict server-side validation.

context.title: Product context
paragraphs: FleetScan (scratch_tracker repository) addresses a real depot workflow where speed and clarity matter more than feature volume.
The MVP covers the driver journey plus a staff overview, while the roadmap openly tracks photos, Realtime, and stronger cache strategy.
bullets: Concrete field constraints shape every UX decision
Scope is deliberate to avoid fake completeness
Roadmap remains technical and product-aligned

architecture.title: Architecture
paragraphs: Routing is organized with `(splash)`, `(public)`, and `(protected)` groups to keep responsibilities explicit.
A feature / controller / view structure drives scan flows, truck dashboard behavior, and fleet overview composition.
bullets: lib/ includes Zod schemas, server actions, and plate/date mappers
Supabase SSR session middleware handles authenticated boundaries
Around 30 Vitest tests cover critical business logic

decisions.title: Key decisions
paragraphs: fr / nl / en localization is cookie-based to keep URLs stable for multi-country field usage.
Zod at the boundaries enforces a strict rule: external data is never trusted by default.
bullets: No over-abstraction: one function per file and isolated features
Camera scanning is isolated in its own dedicated module
Shared RLS rules are versioned and documented in SQL migrations

execution.title: Execution and next steps
paragraphs: The foundation is production-oriented: tests are in place, product documentation exists, and the structure scales without rewrite pressure.
Next steps are explicit: field captures, final create flow completion, and Vercel deployment with monitoring.
bullets: Core architecture is mature enough for hardening
Last-mile delivery is identified, not hidden
Monitoring is planned as part of shipping, not afterthought

shadow.name: Shadow
shadow.tagline: Private communication and planning for a closed circle
shadow.role: Fullstack implementation on Next.js and Supabase: messaging, calendar workflows, PWA setup, and security hardening.

meta.title: Shadow - Case study
meta.description: Private messaging and shared calendar app with Realtime, PWA delivery, RLS, and E2E tests.

paragraphs: A restricted group needs a private communication space with disappearing messages and shared planning, without depending on public consumer tools.
The experience must remain smooth on mobile, install like a native app, and keep technical behavior predictable.
The stack combines Next.js and Supabase with SSR auth, modular CRUD per domain entity, and Realtime updates for threads and badges.
PWA support is production-configured through manifest, service worker, and explicit middleware exclusions to avoid deployment surprises.

context.title: Context
paragraphs: Shadow combines 24-hour ephemeral messaging, direct and group conversations, and an events calendar in one coherent private product.
Fourteen SQL migrations version the data model, and architecture/security documents include pre-deployment audit findings.
bullets: Product scope is coherent, not feature-stacked
Data model evolution is tracked through migrations
Audit output directly informs implementation priorities

architecture.title: Architecture
paragraphs: The codebase is split by feature domains (auth, messages, calendar, home) to preserve ownership clarity.
Entity-level `lib/supabase/CRUD/` modules maintain readability and make integration testing straightforward.
bullets: SWR provides responsive caching behavior for message flows
Supabase Storage handles media payloads
Middleware combines getClaims() checks and auth rate limiting

decisions.title: Decisions
paragraphs: RLS is treated as the primary security boundary, with known pitfalls documented transparently, including conversation access edge cases.
Framer Motion interactions are wrapped with reduced-motion handling for sensitive or high-frequency UX moments.
bullets: Explicit webpack settings ensure PWA and Vercel compatibility
Bundle analysis is integrated to keep performance under control
Five Playwright specs currently secure the auth-critical surface

execution.title: Execution
paragraphs: The MVP is advanced and credible, with final hardening still required (notably CSP and error boundary reinforcement).
It demonstrates a complete technical product lifecycle, not only visual frontend output.
bullets: Core product behavior is stable in real conditions
Remaining security items are known and actionable
Delivery narrative stays honest about what is left

1v1-streetball.name: Take The Crown
1v1-streetball.tagline: Digitizing a 1v1 streetball league without compromising standings integrity
1v1-streetball.role: Fullstack product ownership: player journey (home, games, fight nights, standings), XP/rank progression, organizer dashboard, Supabase model (42 migrations), RLS/RPC security, and EN/FR client documentation.

meta.title: Take The Crown - Case study
meta.description: 1v1 streetball league: standings, player stats, admin dashboard, and secure architecture to guarantee score integrity.

paragraphs: A 1v1 streetball league runs on trust in the standings. Without a proper tool, results spread through messages, stats get lost, and the season becomes hard to arbitrate.
The organizer needs to record games quickly with clear business rules (consistent scores, automatic recalculation). Players want standings, history, and profiles without mobile friction.
The main risk was not visual polish but data integrity: an unprotected direct write can corrupt the entire standings with no operational visibility.
I structured the product around 12 documented business domains, with an explicit critical path: admin record → standings update → realtime player refresh.
Game mutations go only through admin RPC (service_role), never through direct PostgREST writes. RLS, API rate limiting, and Zod validation lock down boundaries.
Assumed trade-off: indie MVP (5–50 beta users) with in-house observability (super_admin error journal) rather than heavy ops stack — suited to volume and budget.

context.title: Product context
paragraphs: Take The Crown serves a local 1v1 basketball league: fight nights, player and spectator (watcher) directory, seasonal standings, progression (XP, ranks, streaks), and public profile pages.
Three roles structure access: player, organizer (admin — record game, drafts, validation), and technical operator (super_admin for platform logs).
The product is mobile-first with fr / en / nl i18n, Europe/Brussels timezone, and a complete client guide (EN + FR) in the repo.

architecture.title: Architecture
paragraphs: Strict separation: app/ (routing), features/ (12 domains), components/ (UI), lib/ (API guard, rate limit, realtime, animations). Thin pages, zero business logic in routes.
ProtectedLeagueRealtimeProvider refreshes lists and dashboard after game changes. unstable_cache 60s on league reads when service_role is configured.
bullets: 42 versioned SQL migrations, RLS on sensitive entities
Admin Server Actions: recordGame, drafts (save/update/finalize), delete
Architecture tests + check:circular-deps + DB/API load scripts
PWA, SEO (sitemap, OG), and documented brand assets

decisions.title: Key decisions
paragraphs: I revoked direct authenticated writes on games (migration 040) after audit: only admin RPC can mutate results.
A senior pre-production audit triggered P0/P1 hardening: GitHub Actions CI, strip debugDetail in prod, HSTS, distributed rate limit on admin actions.
bullets: Prioritize standings integrity before comfort features
In-house error journal vs Sentry — zero cost, MVP volume fit
250 Vitest tests + 11 Playwright E2E (public routes, player journey, admin journey)
CSP, safe open redirect, ILIKE escape on city search

execution.title: Execution and next steps
paragraphs: Code is validated for MVP ship: typecheck, lint, build, and green test suite after post-audit fixes.
Remaining manual actions concern ops (cloud migrations, Vercel env, admin role promotion) — separate from code maturity.
Next: closed beta (5–10 trusted users), then light instrumentation and Vitest coverage percentage.

spill-crumb.name: Spill Crumb
spill-crumb.tagline: Anonymous confessions with realtime feed and platform governance
spill-crumb.role: Fullstack platform architecture and development: auth, paginated feed, synchronized stores, realtime notifications, anti-spam, admin sponsoring/logs, and full CI/E2E pipeline.

meta.title: Spill Crumb - Case study
meta.description: Anonymous social network: feed, reactions, realtime notifications, anti-spam, and admin back-office in production.

paragraphs: The product targets a space where users share stories and confessions under a pseudonym — freedom of expression without exposing real identity.
An anonymous social feed must handle volume, spam, and abuse without weighing down the experience. The 5 posts/day limit and rate limiting are explicit product guardrails.
Admin needs visibility: sponsoring, platform logs, and stats — without opening the codebase to understand system health.
I modeled decoupled post stores (feed, profile, live detail) with centralized fan-out via lib/post-store-sync — one mutation propagates cleanly everywhere.
Supabase realtime is reserved for high-immediate-value interactions (feed, notifications, navbar badge), not applied everywhere by default.
Assumed trade-off: soft launch without mandatory Upstash (in-memory fallback per instance) — documented and recommended before marketing scale campaigns.

context.title: Product context
paragraphs: Spill The Crumbs is available in fr / en / es, responsive across mobile-tablet-desktop, deployed on Vercel from main.
16 product modules cover auth, onboarding, home, posts, profile, notifications, settings, add-new-post, and admin.
MVP is production-ready: email/password auth, stable backend, operational admin tools — with assumed limitations (post reporting, password reset) on the roadmap.

architecture.title: Architecture
paragraphs: ProtectedFeaturePage pattern: centralized auth, typed loaders, i18n errors via AppFeatureError. Each feature follows screen + sections + services/hooks.
Mutations via next-safe-action + Zod. Separate Supabase clients browser / server / admin (service_role never exposed client-side).
bullets: post-feed-store, profile-posts-store, live-store + sync handler registry
post_stats triggers, top drama RPC, Postgres RLS
Upstash rate limit (auth, reactions, comments) + in-memory fallback
638+ Vitest tests, CI deps audit + coverage + local Supabase E2E

decisions.title: Key decisions
paragraphs: The 5 posts/day limit is enforced app-side AND via DB trigger — defense in depth against client bypass.
The /admin/logs dashboard persists platform events with widgets and pagination — lightweight alternative to observability SaaS for MVP.
bullets: Mutation fan-out rather than coupled monolithic stores
Cookie-based i18n without URL segment — 3 locales, en fallback
Husky pre-commit + develop → main pipeline with Vercel previews
Post reporting and verification emails deferred — transparent roadmap

execution.title: Execution and next steps
paragraphs: Product is live in soft launch / internal testing with post-deploy checklist (auth, realtime feed, notifications).
Test coverage (638+ unit, 7 E2E specs) secures evolution on a rich domain (feed, reactions, comments, sponsoring).
Next increments: report moderation, Upstash before scale campaign, password reset and confirmation emails.
I build modern web applications with a product-oriented frontend approach.
I mainly work on SaaS, B2B, and internal tools where frontend execution directly shapes day-to-day use.

productMindset.title: Product mindset
intro: Understand the real need before the technical solution. Decide early, ship focused MVPs, keep a clear trajectory.
No superfluous features: a foundation that can grow without over-complicating too soon.
productMindset.outro: Every decision should answer a concrete use case, not a feature checklist.

frontendPhilosophy.title: Frontend philosophy
intro: Frontend sets a product’s rhythm, fluidity, and perception — not just the screen.
bullets: natural interactions
mobile comfort and smooth flows
visual consistency
details that make usage obvious
frontendPhilosophy.outro: Interfaces that read quickly and move without friction.

buildingApplications.title: What I build
intro: Tools built for real contexts: business workflows, daily use, product logic behind the screen.
bullets: SaaS platforms
business tools
dashboards
internal systems
applications with dense product logic
buildingApplications.outro: Real-world usage guides what we ship, not the other way around.

technicalApproach.title: Technical approach
intro: Simple, component-oriented architectures with coherent UI systems.
bullets: design systems
readable code
scalable foundations
clear separation of concerns
technicalApproach.outro: Technology should serve the product, not the other way around.

motionInteraction.title: Motion & interaction
intro: Animation and micro-interactions for hierarchy and fluidity — never for decoration.
motionInteraction.outro: Discreet, useful motion aligned with the rest of the interface.

collaborationExecution.title: Collaboration & execution
intro: Clear communication, sharp priorities, concrete progress.
bullets: grasp challenges quickly
useful decisions
clean delivery
autonomy without losing structure
collaborationExecution.outro: Serious products, well scoped, shipped cleanly.

closing.label: In short
paragraphs: SaaS, B2B, and modern tools with real product thinking.
Products built to last, evolve, and stay simple to use every day.
